items.q: How do you say 'I'm travelling to Germany' in German?
items.why: Students use 'in' or 'zu'. For most countries and cities: NACH (Deutschland, Berlin, Paris). For countries with articles (die Schweiz, die USA): IN with accusative. Easy to mix up - nach is for proper place names without articles.

A famous faux ami. German 'Gift' = poison; English 'gift' = das Geschenk. The same Germanic root diverged - in English it stayed as 'something given', in German it became specifically a poisonous gift.
Spelled identically to English 'bald' but means SOON. 'Bis bald!' = 'See you soon!'. The English 'bald' translates as 'kahl' or 'glatzköpfig'. Pure faux ami.

items.q: Which sentence correctly says 'I get on well with my mother'?
items.correct: Ich verstehe mich gut mit meiner Mutter.
items.misconception: Ich verstehe mich gut mit meine Mutter.
items.random_wrong: Ich verstehe mir gut mit meiner Mutter.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Ich verstehe mich gut mit meiner Mutter.. “Ich verstehe mich gut mit meine Mutter.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which form of 'you' do you use addressing a German teacher?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sie. “du” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many grammatical cases does standard German have?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 cases. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
